attributes.stem: A 62-year-old woman with severe chronic liver disease, categorised as Child-Pugh C, is diagnosed with oesophageal varices following a screening oesophago-gastroduodenoscopy. The varices are small and there are no red wale marks. She currently takes spironolactone to manage ascites. She is seen by her specialist who notes she is normotensive and that a recent blood test showed a normal serum creatinine level.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management of this patient's varices?
options: Further endoscopic review in six months
attributes.explanation:
This patient has high-risk varices (Child-Pugh C classification and small varices) that require immediate management in the form of primary prophylaxis. A non-selective beta-blocker such as carvedilol or propranolol is the drug of choice for primary prophylaxis of varices in patients with decompensated cirrhosis. The non-selective beta-blockers cause reduced splanchnic blood flow and splanchnic vasoconstriction, resulting in reduced portal pressure. Carvedilol should be avoided in patients with ascites who develop arterial hypotension and/or renal dysfunction.

High-risk varices are also small varices with red wale marks or medium or large varices irrespective of Child-Pugh classification.

Furosemide is incorrect. This would be commenced alongside an anti-mineralocorticoid such as spironolactone in a chronic liver disease patient who has developed ascites. It is not used in the prevention of variceal haemorrhage.

Further endoscopic review in six months is incorrect. This patient is classified as Child-Pugh C and has small varices and therefore falls under the category of "high-risk varices". She needs immediate management of her varices.

Metoprolol is incorrect. This is a selective beta-1-adrenergic receptor inhibitor which has no effect on portal pressure. Therefore, it is not used in the management of varices.

Terlipressin is incorrect. This is a synthetic vasopressin analogue and is used to manage acute variceal bleeding.
learningPoints: A non-selective beta-blocker such as carvidolol or propranolol is the drug of choice for primary prophylaxis of high-risk varices in patients with decompensated cirrhosis.
references: European Association for the Study of the Liver. Electronic address: easloffice@easloffice.eu; European Association for the Study of the Liver. EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines for the management of patients with decompensated cirrhosis. J Hepatol. 2018 Aug;69(2):406-460. doi: 10.1016/j.jhep.2018.03.024. Epub 2018 Apr 10. Erratum in: J Hepatol. 2018 Nov;69(5):1207. PMID: 29653741.
Turco L, Reiberger T, Vitale G, et al. Carvedilol as the new non-selective beta-blocker of choice in patients with cirrhosis and portal hypertension. Liver International. 2023; 43:1183–194.
